analysis.core_story_summary: During satya yuga,there lived a king named Suprateek. Although he had two queens,yet none of them bore any son to him. One day,Suprateek went to meet sage Durvasa,who had his hermitage at the foothills of chitrakut mountain and engaged himself in the sevice of Durvasa.

audience_notes.children_8_12: Use simple language, explain divine names as characters in a sacred legend.
audience_notes.teens_13_17: Connect events to decision-making, identity, and consequences.
audience_notes.young_adults_18_30: Highlight ethics, leadership, and social relevance.
audience_notes.devotional_traditional: Preserve sacred framing and theological terms respectfully.
audience_notes.global_general: Keep cultural context brief and avoid insider assumptions.

sensitivity_notes: Contains conflict imagery that may need age-appropriate framing.
Includes intense mythic elements (curses, demons, destruction).

redesigned_story_by_demographic.children_8_12: **King Durjay — Kids (8-12):** During satya yuga,there lived a king named Suprateek. Although he had two queens,yet none of them bore any son to him. One day,Suprateek went to meet sage Durvasa,who had his hermitage at the foothills of chitrakut mountain and engaged himself in the sevice of Durvasa. This story teaches dedication and sacrifice, divine grace, the cost of conflict. Think of it like a legend that helps us choose kindness, courage, and respect.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.teens_13_17: **King Durjay — Teens (13-17):** During satya yuga,there lived a king named Suprateek. Although he had two queens,yet none of them bore any son to him. One day,Suprateek went to meet sage Durvasa,who had his hermitage at the foothills of chitrakut mountain and engaged himself in the sevice of Durvasa. For teens, the key idea is dedication and sacrifice, divine grace, the cost of conflict. It shows how choices, discipline, and character shape outcomes.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.young_adults_18_30: **King Durjay — Young Adults:** During satya yuga,there lived a king named Suprateek. Although he had two queens,yet none of them bore any son to him. One day,Suprateek went to meet sage Durvasa,who had his hermitage at the foothills of chitrakut mountain and engaged himself in the sevice of Durvasa. A modern takeaway is dedication and sacrifice, divine grace, the cost of conflict. The narrative can be read as a reflection on purpose, responsibility, and values-driven action.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.devotional_traditional: **King Durjay — Devotional/Traditional Readers:** During satya yuga,there lived a king named Suprateek. Although he had two queens,yet none of them bore any son to him. One day,Suprateek went to meet sage Durvasa,who had his hermitage at the foothills of chitrakut mountain and engaged himself in the sevice of Durvasa. The episode highlights dedication and sacrifice, divine grace, the cost of conflict. It preserves reverence for divine agency while encouraging faithful practice and humility.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.global_general: **King Durjay — Global General Audience:** During satya yuga,there lived a king named Suprateek. Although he had two queens,yet none of them bore any son to him. One day,Suprateek went to meet sage Durvasa,who had his hermitage at the foothills of chitrakut mountain and engaged himself in the sevice of Durvasa. In universal terms, it explores dedication and sacrifice, divine grace, the cost of conflict. Cultural names are preserved while the message is presented in accessible contemporary English.
